descriptions.value: “Our journey thus far has been fraught with adventure. The excitement of the hunt is on all occasions courted. Countless herds of elk feed almost undisturbed upon the river bottoms and grizzly Bruin of the mountains is always at hand when wanted.” Tuleries, California Star, 12 April, 1847. Content notice: engage this history with respect and care. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1847.1 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “Three young Indians on the Rancho of Sen’r. Armijo, in Suisun Valley, whilst absent on foot, were attacked and killed by one of those monsters. People should be careful not to travel on foot at this season of the year, in sections of the country which abound with these desperate animals.” Grizzly Bear, The Californian, 15 March, 1848. Content notice: engage this history with respect and care. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1848.1A and T.GB.1848.1B for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The territory of California possesses almost every variety of climate. In the vallies near the coast, spring and summer reign perpetually. The more elevated parts near the mountain tops change from summer’s melting heat to that of winter’s chilling cold. Immediately on the coast the fury of the broad Pacific’s winds keep present almost perpetually Autumn's bracing temperature. The soil of some of the vallies is equal to the richest we have seen in the most fertile portions of the United States, and the remainder is not inferior to the generality of the productive portions of that country. The present vegetable productions are wheat, barley, oats, (wild) corn, potatoes, peas, beans, cabbage, onions, turnips, beets, parsnips, tomatoes, radishes, lettuce, cucumbers, pumpkins, melons, &c. Hemp is found growing wild; cotton is as yet an experiment. The fruits are apples, pears, peaches, nectarines, quinces, figs, prunes, olives, oranges, lemons, grapes, &c. Animals—horses, beef cattle, sheep and goats (local,), grizzly, brown and black bear, elk, deer, antelope, raccoon, rabbit, squirrel, panther, black wolf, prairie wolf, badger, &c. Fish—sturgeon, salmon, mackerel, herring, trout, perch, shark and porpoise, &c. Fowls—geese, ducks, brants, pelicans, swans and a great variety of small birds. Minerals—gold silver, quicksilver, copper. Lead and coal, but little discovered. Woods,—white pine, yellow do., pitch do., sugar do , long leaf do., white and red cedar, red-wood, burr oak, black do., live do., maple, sycamore, black walnut, box elder, alder, willow, madrona, tyon, laurel, ash, cotton-wood, &c. The great peculiarities of this climate are that rain falls plentifully in the winter season, but very seldom in summer,—the almost entire absence of lightning and great rarity of the atmosphere, and the general healthfulness of the country. California will prove a profitable field for the future action of Anglo-Saxon enterprise.” California and its Productions, The Californian, 26 April, 1848. Content notice: engage this history with respect and care. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1848.2A for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The face of the country is generally prairie, clouded here and there with groves of spreading oaks, and. clothed with the richest verdure; among the various grasses clover is found growing spontaneously like thrifty meadows, especially in the damper soils where it attains great perfection, and the grizzly bear during the spring and fore part of summer come down from the mountains and out of the thickets on the river and feed upon it like herds of swine. Wild oats are not so abundant here as in other parts. The rivers are bordered with thickets of various underwood; consisting chiefly of willow, cottonwood, box elder, alder, white ash and sycamore, all thickly interwoven with grape vines.” For the Californian, The Californian, 26 April, 1848. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1848.2A for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

Scientists, explorers, and government officials in the American West

descriptions.value: “Col. Fremont's Geographical Memoir on Upper California thus speaks of the climate of the gold region: The gold region of California is on the Sacramento river and its tributaries. The climate of the country has no winter in the valley, but the rainy season and the dry. The rainy season begins in November and continues to the middle of February or the beginning of March; the rest of the year is without rain; but the streams from the Sierra Nevada afford all the facilities for irrigation in the heats of July and August. The whole valley abounds in wild cattle, wild horses, elks, deer, antelopes, grizzly bears, partridges, water-fowI, salmon, &c. All the products of the United States, from apples to oranges, from potatoes to sugarcane, may be produced in the valley of the San Joaquin and Sacramento. The climate is remarkably healthy. Such is the California on the Pacific; the richest, most picturesque and beautiful region, for its extent, upon the face of the earth Such is the Eldorado of the gold mines; such is the great acquisition of the late war with Mexico. The California gold-finding scheme turns out, it is said, to be merely a hoax or rather, a delusion. The imaginary gold is but a very common valueless mineral, yellow mica. Many of our early voyagers were deceived the same way. Liverpool Mercury. That will be news indeed when it reaches California. Those who cannot tell yellow mica from gold could hardly find their way to that out-of-the-way country. Perhaps when the “Wilmot Proviso” extends to California, the gold miners will become more enlightened — New York Sun, The California Gold.” The California Gold, Weekly Alta California, 1 March, 1849. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1849.1 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

properties.SocialGroup: Scientists, explorers, and government officials in the American West

descriptions.value: “A Great Vision made Reality. In 1841 a very distinguished orator, in the Senate of the United States, in a speech upon the Pre-emption Bill, uttered the following prophetic language. What was prediction then, is about to become history now. “If to the other ties that bind us together as one people, he superadded the powerful interest springing out of' a just administration of our exhaustless public domain —by which, for a long succession of ages, in seasons of peace, the states will enjoy the benefit of the great and growing revenue which it produces, and in periods of war that revenue will be applied to the prosecution of the war—we shall be forever linked together with the strength of adamantine chains. No section, no state, would ever be mad enough to break off from the Union, and deprive itself of the inestimable advantages which it secures. Although thirty or forty more of the new states should be admitted into this Union, this measure would cement them all fast together. The honorable member from Missouri near me, (Mr. Linn,) is very anxious to have a settlement formed at the mouth of the Oregon, and he will probably be satisfied at no very distant day. Then will be seen members from the Pacific States scaling the Rocky Mountains, passing the country of the grizzly bear, descending the turbid Missouri, entering the father of rivers, ascending the beautiful Ohio, and coming to this capitol to take their seats in its spacious and magnificent halls. Proud of the commission they bear, and happy to find themselves here in council with friends and brothers and countrymen, enjoying the incalculable advantages of the great confederacy, and among them their annual distributive share of the is sues of a nation’s inheritance, would even they, the remote people of the Pacific, ever desire to separate themselves from such a high and glorious destiny?”.” A Great Vision Made Reality, Daily Pacific News, 15 November, 1849. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1849.2 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

Hunters of the American West

descriptions.value: “Correspondence of the Pacific News. J Woods’ Creek, (Stanislaus River,) December 2,1849. Provisions are high here at present. Flour, pork, bread, rice, sugar, &c. $1.00 per Fresh beef and venison, 37 & cts. Venison can be had in abundance. Bear meat (grizzly) is selling for 50 cts per lb. The snow is on the mountains north and east of us but there has been none here as yet. There has been some slight frost for the last 7 or 8 nights, but the days are warm. There has not been rain enough to keep the small ravines running. J.” Correspondence of the Pacific News, Daily Pacific News, 13 December, 1849. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1849.1 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

SocialGroup: Miners and prospectors of the American West
Hunters of the American West

descriptions.value: “The news from the mines, of which I have dates of but a few hours past, is quite encouraging. Miners, generally, are doing tolerably well. Some new gulches have been opened which promise to be as rich as Sullivan’s was; and probably is as rich as any that have been previously discovered. There has been a report quite prevalent here for several days past, and generally believed, that a gulch recently opened by three men, 20 miles eastward of the Sonoranian camp, yielded, in three days work, twenty-seven pounds of gold! Another report is that a gulch opened on the Stanislaus river, was yielding, to the lucky miners, eight, ten and twelve ounces per day! These reports have no substantiated authority, but I learned, in a conversation with a gentleman just down from Woods’s Diggings, that he, in part, accredited the latter report from information given him by a friend, in whom he places the greatest confidence. The health of the miners is very good; and, as near as I can learn, the miners are wearing away the winter very comfortably and profitably, notwithstanding the high price of everything requisite to sustain life. Many of them have built log cabins and are enjoying their dishes of venison, grizzly bear and other game; and all, who have come from the mines recently, give their preference to a residence in the mountains during the winter, to a locality in this place or San Francisco. The large streams and creeks have been temporarily abandoned by the miners in quest of dry diggings, and it will be not at all surprising, if before Spring, the people of the country are electrified with accounts of richer diggings than even the most credulous have entertained an idea of. More anon.” Later from Stockton and the Mines, Daily Pacific News, 1 January, 1850. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1849.2 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “An enormous grizzly bear, weighing from 1000 to 1100 pounds, was brought into the city yesterday. This monster was taken about 60 miles up the Sacramento. The captors first lodged a ball in his heart, which brought him down; but before venturing to make his personal acquaintance, policy dictated that his physiogomy would be a proper receptacle for a second visitation. He was very fat, and the delicacies as they were cut off and retailed, sold for $1 a pound — thus putting considerable money in the pockets of the captors.” GB.1850.1, Daily Alta California, 7 February, 1850. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for ___________ for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The San Andreas Independent states that two hunters who killed a grizzly bear on Bear Mountain, some two weeks ago, realized quite a handsome reward from the adventure. Besides the meat, which at twenty cents a pound was worth $120, they sold the gall to some Chinese for $30. The Celestials sent it to China, to be used as an agent in coloring.” GB.1859.1, Petaluma Weekly Argus, 6 January, 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1859.1 for full context and citations. Content notice: engage this history with respect and care. descriptions.value: “The presence of numerous grizzly bears near town is reported; the heavy fall of snow in the Sierra Nevadas has driven the deer, bear, and other large game down to the snowless region.” T.GB.1859.1, San Joaquin Republican, 20 January, 1860. descriptions.value: “The Independent says: “Hunters report that quite a number of the dangerous customers have their lairs among the chnpparcl and rocks north-east of Salt Spring Valley, and not over three miles from San Andreas. The late heavy falls of snow in the Sierra Nevada mountains, have driven the deer, bear and all other large game down to the snowless region, and our hunters are enjoying a rich harvest of sport.” T.GB.1859.2, Daily National Democrat, 23 November, 1859.
